label: Moscow
sub: Capital of Russia since 1918; former Muscovite principality seat from 14th century. First mentioned in chronicles in 1147.

label: St. Petersburg
sub: Founded by Peter the Great in 1703 on conquered Swedish territory; Russian imperial capital 1712–1918 (also called Petrograd 1914–24, Leningrad 1924–91).

label: Kyiv
sub: Capital of Kievan Rus from 882; called 'mother of all Russian cities' by Oleg. Now capital of Ukraine and front-line in the 2022 Russian invasion.

label: Veliky Novgorod
sub: Legendary seat of Rurik (862). Novgorod Republic was a powerful medieval state until absorbed by Moscow in 1478. First center of Russian statehood.

label: Vladimir
sub: Capital of Vladimir-Suzdal principality after Kyiv's decline (12th–13th c.); Grand Duchy of Vladimir seat before Moscow's rise.

label: Kazan
sub: Capital of the Kazan Khanate; conquered by Ivan the Terrible in 1552 after a siege — a pivotal Russian eastward expansion. Now capital of Tatarstan.

label: Volgograd (Stalingrad)
sub: Site of the Battle of Stalingrad (Aug 1942–Feb 1943) — turning point of WWII. Soviet victory over German 6th Army; 1.5M+ casualties. Renamed Volgograd in 1961.

label: Sevastopol
sub: Strategic naval base on the Black Sea; Russian imperial fortress since 1783. Russia illegally annexed Crimea in 2014; Sevastopol hosts the Black Sea Fleet.

label: Grozny
sub: Capital of Chechnya; almost totally destroyed in two Chechen Wars (1994–96, 1999–2009). Symbol of Russian counter-insurgency brutality and eventual reconsolidation.

label: Kursk
sub: Site of Battle of Kursk (July 1943) — largest tank battle in history; decisive Soviet victory. In 2024, Ukrainian forces launched a cross-border incursion into Kursk Oblast.

label: Mariupol
sub: Port city besieged for 87 days in 2022; fell to Russia on May 16. Estimated 20,000+ civilians died. Azovstal steel plant was last Ukrainian holdout.

label: Bakhmut
sub: Site of the longest and bloodiest battle of the Ukraine war (Aug 2022–May 2023). Captured by Wagner Group forces on May 20, 2023 after months of grinding urban combat.

label: Bucha
sub: Town near Kyiv where Russian forces committed documented massacres of civilians in March 2022. ICC investigations ongoing; Russia denies all allegations.

label: Vladivostok
sub: Russia's principal Pacific port, founded 1860 on territory acquired from China. Hosts the Pacific Fleet. Gateway to Asia; site of Eastern Economic Forum meetings.

label: Yekaterinburg
sub: Industrial Ural city where the Romanov imperial family was executed on July 17, 1918. Boris Yeltsin's hometown. Cathedral on the Blood built on the execution site.

label: Chelyabinsk / Mayak
sub: Closed Soviet nuclear city; site of the 1957 Kyshtym disaster at the Mayak plutonium facility — the third-worst nuclear accident in history (INES 6). Preceded Chernobyl by 29 years.

label: Semipalatinsk Test Site
sub: Soviet primary nuclear weapons test site in Kazakhstan; site of first Soviet nuclear test (RDS-1, Aug 29, 1949). 456 nuclear tests conducted 1949–1989. Now in Kazakhstan.

label: Arkhangelsk
sub: Russia's first major seaport, founded 1584; vital WWII supply route for Allied Arctic convoys. Near Plesetsk Cosmodrome and Nenoksa nuclear testing facility.

label: Murmansk
sub: Russia's only ice-free Arctic port; largest city above the Arctic Circle. Home to Northern Fleet nuclear submarines and critical Arctic geopolitical competition.

label: Novosibirsk
sub: Russia's third-largest city; Siberia's major industrial and scientific hub. Founded 1893 during Trans-Siberian Railway construction. Home to Akademgorodok science city.

label: Irkutsk
sub: Major Siberian city founded 1652; gateway to Lake Baikal (world's deepest lake). Cossack fort on the eastward push to the Pacific. Key Trans-Siberian Railway junction.

label: Chernobyl
sub: Site of April 26, 1986 nuclear explosion — worst nuclear disaster in history. 350,000+ evacuated; exclusion zone of 2,600 km². Located in northern Ukraine. Briefly occupied by Russian forces in 2022.

label: Kharkiv
sub: Ukraine's second-largest city; former Soviet capital of Ukraine 1919–34. Subject to intense Russian missile and drone attacks throughout the war; Ukrainian forces repelled 2022 assault.

label: Zaporizhzhia NPP
sub: Europe's largest nuclear power plant; seized by Russian forces on March 4, 2022. Under IAEA monitoring. Multiple safety incidents due to combat; external power supply repeatedly severed.

label: Donetsk
sub: Capital of the self-declared Donetsk People's Republic (2014). De facto Russian-held since 2014; formally annexed by Russia in September 2022 though full oblast not under Russian control.

label: Luhansk
sub: Capital of the self-declared Luhansk People's Republic (2014). Formally annexed by Russia in September 2022; nearly all oblast now under Russian control.

label: Simferopol (Crimea)
sub: Administrative center of Crimea. Russian 'little green men' seized key installations in February 2014; disputed referendum on March 16 led to annexation. Status internationally disputed.

label: Minsk
sub: Capital of Belarus; near Viskuly where Belavezha Accords dissolved USSR (Dec 8, 1991). Under Lukashenko a key Russian ally; hosts Minsk Agreements (2014–15) on Donbas.

label: Kherson
sub: Regional capital captured by Russia in March 2022; liberated by Ukraine on November 11, 2022. Russia retains eastern bank of Dnipro River; city under near-constant shelling.

label: Baikonur Cosmodrome
sub: Soviet/Russian space launch facility in Kazakhstan; site of Sputnik (1957), Gagarin's flight (1961), and all Soviet/Russian crewed missions. Leased from Kazakhstan until 2050.

label: Kaliningrad
sub: Russian exclave entirely surrounded by NATO members Poland and Lithuania; the former German city of Königsberg, home of Immanuel Kant. Transferred to USSR in 1945. Now hosts Russia's Baltic Fleet and Iskander-M ballistic missile systems, making it a major NATO flashpoint.

label: Tbilisi
sub: Capital of Georgia; site of the August 2008 Russo-Georgian War. Russian forces advanced to within 40km of Tbilisi before ceasing operations. Russia recognized South Ossetia and Abkhazia as independent — the first post-Soviet territorial acquisition and a precedent for future interventions.

label: Smolensk / Katyn
sub: Historic western fortress city near the Katyn Forest, site of the Katyn massacre (April–May 1940): Soviet NKVD executed ~22,000 Polish military officers, police, and intellectuals on Stalin's orders. The USSR blamed Germany until 1990. Russia officially acknowledged responsibility in 2010 under Medvedev.

label: Poltava
sub: Site of the Battle of Poltava (July 8, 1709): Peter the Great decisively defeated Charles XII of Sweden and his Ukrainian Cossack ally Ivan Mazepa. The victory secured Russian dominance over Eastern Europe and marked Russia's emergence as a major European great power.

label: Rostov-on-Don
sub: Russia's gateway to the Caucasus and key logistics hub for the Ukraine war. On June 24, 2023, Wagner Group forces under Prigozhin seized the city's Southern Military District headquarters during a 24-hour armed rebellion — the most serious internal security challenge to Putin since the 1993 constitutional crisis.

label: Kerch Bridge
sub: 19km bridge connecting Crimea to Russia's Krasnodar Krai, completed May 2018 under Putin's personal oversight — the only physical link between Russia and Crimea. Ukraine struck it in October 2022 and July 2023, causing structural damage and disrupting Russian logistics.

label: Odessa
sub: Ukraine's main Black Sea port, founded 1794 under Catherine the Great and long-contested as a Russian cultural city. Russia blockaded Odessa's grain exports in 2022 (triggering a global food crisis), repeatedly struck port infrastructure, and periodically threatened amphibious assault.

label: Almaty
sub: Kazakhstan's largest city; site of January 2022 uprising over fuel prices that escalated into mass anti-government protests. Russia-led CSTO deployed 2,500 troops at President Tokayev's request — the first use of the collective security mechanism to suppress domestic unrest in a partner state.

label: Brest (Brest-Litovsk)
sub: Site of the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk (March 3, 1918) between Soviet Russia and the Central Powers. Russia ceded Ukraine, Finland, the Baltic states, and vast territories — losing a quarter of its European territory. Lenin justified the 'obscene peace' as necessary to consolidate Bolshevik power.

label: Khabarovsk
sub: Russia's Far Eastern capital; site of the largest sustained provincial protests in Russian history (summer 2020) after popular Governor Sergei Furgal was arrested on alleged murder charges many locals believed were politically motivated. Tens of thousands marched weekly, reflecting deep anti-Kremlin sentiment in Russia's distant east.
